food_subgroup: Cooked fish dish with spiced gravy

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for fish tikka masala; closest parent 'fish dish' (FOODON_03302051) used as ontology anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 term exists for fish tikka masala specifically; 'Fish, cooked' (A0CKP) used as closest parent. The dish involves tandoor-grilled fish pieces finished in a spiced onion-tomato gravy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for fish tikka masala or a sufficiently close parent food concept.

main_ingredients: white fish (e.g. basa, tilapia, or cod)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of fish tikka masala is approximately 350 g, comprising grilled fish pieces in a rich spiced onion-tomato-cream gravy, served with rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Tikka Masala and analogous Indian fish curry data — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Fish, mixed species, cooked (NDB 15264) and Indian curry sauce components
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition) and USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for fish tikka masala exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA databases. Values are estimated by combining ICMR-NIN data for Indian fish curry (Machli Curry, ICMR-NIN 2017, p. 94) with USDA SR Legacy cooked white fish data and standard North Indian masala gravy composition. The dish is approximately 55% fish and 45% gravy by weight. Energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate values are weighted composites. Sodium is elevated due to salt in marinade and gravy. All values are per 100 g of the finished dish as served.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed Indian fish curry and analogous protein-dominant curry dishes — University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson FS et al. (2021) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for fish tikka masala. GI estimated at 35 based on analogous protein-dominant Indian curry dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database. High-protein, low-carbohydrate dishes with spiced tomato-onion gravy consistently show GI in the 30–40 range. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 18.2) / 100 ≈ 6.4, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 5.2 × 3.5 = 18.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1470 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a full restaurant portion of fish tikka masala may contribute approximately 60–65% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced salt in the marinade and gravy preparation is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and moderate carbohydrate content (~5.2 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fish tikka masala has a low glycemic index and a moderate glycemic load per serving (~6.4), making it a suitable protein-rich main dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when paired with a low-GI carbohydrate such as basmati rice or whole-wheat roti in controlled portions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from cream and dairy marinade (~2.1 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While fish provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids and lean protein, the cream-based gravy contributes saturated fat and the dish is high in sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should request reduced cream and salt, and limit portion size to 200–250 g.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fish Tikka Masala is a North Indian restaurant dish consisting of boneless fish pieces — typically a firm white fish such as basa, tilapia, or cod — that are first marinated in spiced yoghurt (with ginger, garlic, cumin, coriander, turmeric, and chilli), then grilled in a tandoor (clay oven) to achieve a lightly charred, smoky exterior. The grilled fish tikka pieces are subsequently simmered in a rich onion-tomato-cream gravy seasoned with garam masala and finished with fresh coriander. The result is a dish with tender, moist fish, a mildly spiced and aromatic sauce, and a characteristic orange-red colour from tomato and spices. Nutritionally, fish tikka masala is a high-protein, moderate-fat dish with a low glycemic index (~35), making it suitable for blood glucose management. The fish component provides lean protein and omega-3 fatty acids, while the cream and oil in the gravy contribute saturated fat. Sodium content is elevated due to the marinade and gravy seasoning, which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. In Singapore, this dish is commonly served at North Indian restaurants and is popular as a main course paired with naan, roti, or basmati rice. It is halal-certified at most Indian Muslim establishments and is gluten-free by default when served without bread.

meal_context: paired with naan or basmati rice

ingredient_synonyms.tandoor: traditional clay oven used in North Indian cooking

confidence_reason: No direct entry for fish tikka masala exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN Indian fish curry data and USDA SR Legacy cooked white fish data combined with standard North Indian masala gravy composition. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on comparable protein-dominant Indian curry dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level data sources are approved primary sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN Indian fish curry entry or the USDA SR Legacy fish data used for this estimation. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary source data used for this estimation. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source used. All other -1 values reflect nutrients not reported in the composite source data. The food_name has been normalised from the input 'Fish Tikka Masala (Chef Recommendation)' to the canonical form 'Fish Tikka Masala'; the chef recommendation qualifier is a menu descriptor and not part of the canonical food identity. The restaurant serving size of 350 g is an estimate based on typical North Indian restaurant portion norms; actual serving weight at Aryana Restaurant may vary. Halal status is flagged as likely true for this North Indian Muslim restaurant context but should be verified against the restaurant's halal certification. GI category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule (GI = 35).
